text: Welcome to another installment of robotic hernia surgery. Today, we're going to be reviewing a case of robotic femoral hernia repair in a woman. This was a woman in her seventies who presented with chronic groin pain and a bulge. She had had an ultrasound performed by her PCP, which demonstrated findings consistent with a femoral hernia. In terms of case setup for inguinal hernias done robotically, I like to have my ports triangulated to the intended groin. I like to also make sure that I'm off midline so I don't put any large trochars through a diastasis or the linea alba, reducing potential for incisional hernia.

Upon entry and docking, the robotic instruments reveal a femoral hernia. The surgeon creates a standard peritoneal flap and begins medial dissection towards the pubis. The lateral dissection initially occurs in an incorrect plane but is adjusted to access the visceral compartment of the preperitoneal space. The surgeon progresses towards the femoral hernia, preparing for its reduction by developing retropubic and lateral dissections.

The surgeon focuses on the femoral hernia, which contains preperitoneal fat and lymphatics. The hernia sac is visible, favoring the medial side of the dissection to avoid injury to the femoral vein. The surgeon reduces the chronic incarcerated fat from the space, revealing a pseudo stack appearance.

The surgeon carefully dissects the obturator space, reducing pre-peritoneal fat while avoiding over-dissection to prevent lymphocele formation. The obturator canal is identified and dissected to ensure adequate mesh coverage.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the myoperitoneal orifice, transects the round ligament, and parietalizes surrounding tissue to create a space for mesh placement against the lateral sidewall. The iliopsoas muscle, femoral vein, and obturator nerve are identified, confirming the absence of an indirect hernia. A contoured mesh is prepared for placement.

The surgeon prepares a 15 by 10 centimeter mid-weight polypropylene mesh for fixation at Cooper's ligament. Hemostasis is achieved using bipolar cautery to prevent bleeding during suture placement. The peritoneal flap is closed from lateral to medial using an absorbable V-lock suture.

Straightforward case. These are all, of course, outpatient procedures, and this patient did very well. Again, femoral hernias in women. Gold standard now is a MIS repair. I hope that this was helpful. Thank you.
